interactions.summary: Jiang completes the logical critique of tomb theory, then acknowledges tomb-supporting evidence in a Pharaoh's instruction about the house of the dead being for life.
He surveys a Tesla-style battery theory that reads the pyramid as tuned to Earth and built to store clean energy, but rejects it because it does not work scientifically and lacks a plausible use.

Jiang offers his alternative: the Great Pyramid as Egypt's Manhattan Project, a collective attempt to harness divine power, end history, and use the Pharaoh's sacred body as a portal between heaven and earth.

He moves inside the grand gallery from the worshipper's point of view: prayer draws star energy through the mummy, turning the pyramid into a divine battery and the gallery into a nexus of life/death, womb/tomb, heaven/earth, myth/reality.

claims.claim: Jiang's third objection to tomb theory is practical: if a pyramid takes decades to build, a Pharaoh whose afterlife depends on it cannot know he will live long enough to complete it.
claims.temporal_scope: Lecture claim dated 2024-11-26 about tomb theory.

claims.claim: The lecture acknowledges textual evidence for a tomb reading: the Pharaoh's grave in the West is described as the house of the dead that makes life or immortality possible.
claims.temporal_scope: Lecture claim dated 2024-11-26 about evidence used by tomb theorists.

claims.claim: Jiang presents the Tesla battery theory as more plausible than a simple tomb theory but ultimately inadequate: it treats the pyramid as storing natural energy while failing scientifically and leaving unclear what the energy powered.
claims.temporal_scope: Lecture claim dated 2024-11-26 about alternative pyramid theories.

claims.claim: Jiang's central speculative model is that the Great Pyramid was Egypt's Manhattan Project: a vast collective attempt to harness the power of God, create eternal peace on earth, and end pain, suffering, death, and history.
In this model, the Pharaoh's mummy is not incidental: the sacred body is the portal or mechanism through which Egyptians communicate with the ascended Pharaoh and channel his power on earth.
Jiang reworks the battery theory by replacing clean energy with divine energy: worship in the grand gallery draws star energy through the Pharaoh and reflects it across Egypt.

The grand gallery is interpreted as a ritual nexus: the dead Pharaoh is alive for faith, the mortal worshipper communicates with God, and the same dark passage reads as womb and tomb, heaven and earth, myth and reality.

signature_moments.moment: The Great Pyramid becomes Egypt's Manhattan Project, built to harness God and bring an end to history.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the Great Pyramid was designed as Egypt's Manhattan Project
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture's central metaphor and gives the public read its argumentative spine.

signature_moments.moment: Tesla's battery is transformed into a religious machine: free energy becomes divine energy.
signature_moments.source_phrase: The pyramid was a battery to channel divine energy in order to power Egypt
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves Jiang's method of converting a fringe material theory into a symbolic-political model.

signature_moments.moment: The grand gallery is both womb and tomb, both birth passage and burial chamber.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a nexus of being born and dying
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives the ritual experience its force and prevents the read from flattening the pyramid into an abstract institution.

signature_moments.moment: The pyramid is a collision of heaven and earth, myth and reality.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a collision, a nexus of myth and reality
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It names the recurring Jiang primitive: a constructed object forces invisible myth into visible social reality.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's central metaphor for the pyramid: a massive state-religious project to channel divine power and create eternal peace.
A reworked battery theory in which worship channels divine rather than electrical energy through the Pharaoh and across Egypt.
Jiang's term for ritual compression points where life/death, birth/burial, heaven/earth, and myth/reality occupy one space.

uncertainty_notes.note: The Tesla battery material is reported as a theory inside Jiang's lecture; no independent verification is attempted in this packet pass.
Transcript says 'the Jews believed he was divine'; context suggests Jiang likely means Egyptians, but the packet preserves the source wording and marks the model as speculative.
